frame_label: Misogyny Jokes: Childbearing/Rearing as Women's Responsibility
generated_comment:
A: ...my in-laws are not very happy about the PhD, they think it's not the right time.
B: Oh, I understand, dear. When I had a similar chance, I decided against it. My in-laws had concerns, and honestly, I realised they weren't wrong. Once you have children, those early years just fly by, and who will look after them properly if you're both away studying or working? Career can wait a bit, but those childhood memories, 'bachpan', they don't come back, you know? It's a mother's instinct to want to be there.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment is misogynistic because it covertly pressures the younger colleague to prioritize child-rearing over career ambition, framing it as a natural maternal duty ('mother's instinct') and suggesting that a woman's career 'can wait' while childhood 'doesn't come back'. It exemplifies the 'expecting the mother to sacrifice career' and 'saying who will raise the children?' manifestations of the F03 frame, drawing on the speaker's own past decision and family expectations.
original_frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
persona_text: You are Priya. Age 38. Married. A woman working as a schoolteacher in Kolkata. You are in the staff room with a younger female colleague who just got admitted to a PhD programme at a university in another city. The colleague is excited but nervous Ñ her husband is supportive but her in laws are not happy. She is asking you for advice. You are giving it. Your advice is rooted in your own experience Ñ you gave up a similar opportunity 10 years ago because the timing was not right and the family was not ready. What you tell her and how you frame what you gave up reveals how you have processed that decision over 10 years and whether you have made peace with it or are still living inside it.
